title: Plum frangipane cake
subtitle: A sweet pastry case filled with almond cream and plums
excerpt: This tart-style cake combines a crisp sweet shortcrust case with a rich frangipane filling and halved plums pressed in before baking. The frangipane puffs around the fruit as it bakes, setting to a moist, tender almond layer that holds the plums in place. It serves 8 to 10 as a dessert. The total time, including chilling the pastry, is about 2 hours.
sourceNotes: Frangipane tarts with stone fruit are central to French regional patisserie, appearing in the Bourdaloue tart (pear frangipane), the Bakewell tart (a British adaptation with cherry jam) and dozens of seasonal variants. The pairing with plums is common in the Dordogne and Languedoc regions of southern France. Jane Grigson documents frangipane-filled tarts in 'Good Things' (1971); Richard Bertinet gives a definitive method in 'Pastry' (2012).

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The pastry case can be blind-baked up to a day ahead and stored at room temperature. The frangipane can be made and refrigerated up to 2 days ahead.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; blind-bake the case at 190 degrees C, then reduce to 180 degrees C for the filled tart

glossaryTerms.definition: A mixture of ground almonds, butter, sugar and eggs beaten together to a thick, smooth cream, used as a filling for tarts and cakes. When baked, frangipane puffs slightly and sets to a moist, tender almond layer that holds fruit in place without it sinking to the base. The key is creaming the butter and sugar thoroughly before adding the eggs, to prevent the mixture from splitting. If it does split, beat in a tablespoon of the ground almonds before continuing.

content.text: The two elements of this cake can be made at different times. The pastry case needs to be blind-baked first, then the
filling and plums go in for the second bake. Do not skip the blind bake: a raw pastry case will not crisp under a wet almond filling.

Use firm plums that will hold their shape through the bake, such as Victoria or Marjorie's Seedling. Very ripe or overripe plums will collapse into the frangipane and make the filling wet. Press the plums in cut-side up so they caramelise on their flat face during baking.

Rub the 100 g cold butter into the 200 g flour and 40 g icing sugar until the mixture resembles fine breadcrumbs. Mix in the egg yolk and enough ice-cold water (1 to 2 tbsp) to bring it together into a dough. Flatten into a disc, wrap and chill for 30 minutes.

Heat the oven to 190 degrees C fan. Roll the pastry out and line a loose-bottomed 23 cm tart tin. Prick the base, line with baking paper and fill with baking beans. Blind-bake for 15 minutes, then remove the paper and beans and bake for a further 5 minutes until the base is pale golden and dry. Reduce the oven to 180 degrees C.

Beat the 100 g softened butter and 100 g caster sugar together until pale and fluffy. Beat in the 2 eggs one at a time, then fold in the 100 g ground almonds, 15 g flour and 0.5 tsp almond extract. Spread into the blind-baked case. Arrange the 400 g plum halves cut-side up, pressing them gently into the frangipane. Sprinkle with 15 g caster sugar. Bake at 180 degrees C fan for 35 to 40 minutes until the frangipane is puffed, golden brown, and set with a slight wobble only at the very centre. Cool in the tin before slicing.

items.solution: The blind-bake step was skipped or shortened. The case must be baked to dry before the frangipane is added, or the moisture from the filling will prevent it from crisping. If the base looks pale after removing the beans, return it to the oven for a further 5 minutes.

The plums had too much surface moisture. Pat the cut faces dry with kitchen paper before pressing them into the frangipane. Very juicy plums can also be macerated with a teaspoon of sugar first to draw off excess liquid.

The butter and eggs were at different temperatures. The butter must be genuinely soft before creaming. If the mixture splits, beat in a tablespoon of the ground almonds before adding the remaining eggs.
